summary: Fission Bio is developing small molecule inhibitors targeting mitochondrial dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases. The company has raised $400,000 through Cerebrum DAO to advance SC9, a drug candidate derived from Stanford's P110 peptide that blocks pathological Drp1-Fis1 interaction. The document describes 13 years of validation across 11 disease models and the transition from peptide to small molecule development.

content_blocks.title: Company Overview and Problem Statement
content_blocks.content: Fission Bio addresses mitochondrial dysfunction in brain disease. Neurons have high energy demands requiring hundreds of mitochondria per cell. Mitochondrial fragmentation and failure actively drives diseases like Alzheimer's, ALS, and Huntington's (citations: Qi et al., 2013; Guo et al., 2013). The company raised $400,000 through Cerebrum DAO's IP-Token model to develop drugs targeting the fundamental mechanism of cellular energy breakdown rather than downstream symptoms.

content_blocks.title: Hero Image
content_blocks.content: Microscopy image of mitochondrial network with overlay text 'Fixing Broken Mitochondria to Stop Brain Disease'

content_blocks.title: Biological Mechanism of Mitochondrial Dysfunction
content_blocks.content: Healthy mitochondria balance fusion (merging into networks) and fission (dividing for energy demands and damage removal). Neurodegenerative diseases disrupt this balance. During inflammatory stress, protein Drp1 interacts with Fis1 receptor triggering excessive mitochondrial fragmentation while fusion decreases (Guo et al., 2013; Joshi et al., 2018). Result is fragmented, dysfunctional energy producers unable to meet metabolic demands. Mitochondrial dysfunction directly linked to progression of Alzheimer's (cognitive decline, neuronal death), ALS, Huntington's, and Parkinson's disease.

content_blocks.title: Electron Microscopy of Mitochondria in Alzheimer's Model
content_blocks.content: Series of electron microscopy images from Zhang et al., 2016 showing mitochondria in brain tissue from Alzheimer's disease model mice. Images labeled d1, d2, d3 show mitochondria undergoing splitting. Image e shows abnormal mitochondrial shapes and disrupted internal structures. Scale bars: 2 μm and 0.5 μm. Demonstrates pathological mitochondrial morphology in disease models.

content_blocks.title: DRP1-Dependent Disease Map
content_blocks.content: Body diagram showing diseases driven by DRP1-dependent mitochondrial fission across multiple organ systems: BRAIN (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, Multiple Sclerosis, AMD, ALS), STROKE, HEART & LUNGS (congestive heart failure, COPD, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis), GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT (IBD), SEPSIS. Indicates broad therapeutic potential of mechanism.

content_blocks.title: P110 Discovery and Validation
content_blocks.content: Stanford researchers developed P110 peptide to block pathological Drp1-Fis1 interaction while preserving normal mitochondrial dynamics (Qi et al., 2013). 13 years of research across 11 disease models demonstrated therapeutic potential: reduced neurodegeneration in Huntington's disease models (Guo et al., 2013), restored mitochondrial function in ALS patient cells (Joshi et al., 2018), improved cognitive performance in Alzheimer's models (Joshi et al., 2018), enhanced cardiac function in ischemic disease (Tian et al., 2017). P110 achieved benefits without causing immunosuppression - major limitation of anti-inflammatory treatments. However, peptides face therapeutic obstacles: complex manufacturing, poor brain penetration, rapid degradation.

content_blocks.title: SC9 Small Molecule Development
content_blocks.content: Dr. Luis Rios (Co-Founder and Principal Investigator) identified specific binding site termed SWAG (switch I-adjacent groove) where P110 interferes with Fis1 interaction. Team developed SC9, a small molecule mimicking P110's protective effects while overcoming peptide limitations (Rios et al., 2023). Represents transition from peptide to druggable small molecule.

content_blocks.title: Funding Model and Progress
content_blocks.content: Cerebrum DAO funded initial $200,000 development phase. IP tokenized through MITY tokens on Molecule platform, raising additional $188,000 for lead identification and in-vivo testing. Total raised: $388,000 (note: intro states $400,000). IP-Token model advantages: direct community investment, transparent development with regular updates, shared IP ownership through tokens, faster decision-making without institutional bureaucracy. MITY token holders fund, govern, and guide progress while owning fractional stakes in patents. Model addresses 'valley of death' between laboratory discoveries and viable treatments.

content_blocks.title: Social Media Engagement
content_blocks.content: Screenshot of FissionBio Twitter post from Aug 27, 2025 promoting podcast with Dr. Luis Rios on Cerebrum DAO's Meet The Scientists discussing mitochondria, fission, and DeSci funding advantages. Shows active community engagement and scientific communication.

content_blocks.title: Current Stage and Next Steps
content_blocks.content: Fission Bio has proven concept works in laboratory models. Current stage: systematic process of turning promising compounds into medicines. Community participates in development process through Cerebrum DAO tokenization model. Links provided for MITY tokens, project details, and DAO participation.

content_blocks.title: Key References Part 1
content_blocks.content: Guo et al. JCI 2013 - P110 inhibition diminishes Huntington's neurodegeneration. Joshi et al. EMBO Mol Med 2018 - Drp1/Fis1 inhibition slows ALS progression. Joshi et al. Oncotarget 2018 - Drp1/Fis1 mediates mitochondrial dysfunction and cognitive decline in Alzheimer's.

content_blocks.title: Key References Part 2
content_blocks.content: Qi et al. J Cell Sci 2013 - Novel Drp1 inhibitor (P110) diminishes aberrant mitochondrial fission and neurotoxicity. Rios et al. Nat Commun 2023 - Targeting allosteric site in Drp1 to inhibit Fis1-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction (SC9 development). Tian et al. J Mol Med 2017 - Ischemia-induced Drp1/Fis1-mediated mitochondrial fission in pulmonary hypertension.

key_findings: 13 years of validation across 11 disease models for P110 peptide including Huntington's, ALS, Alzheimer's, and cardiac ischemia
SC9 small molecule developed to overcome peptide limitations (poor brain penetration, rapid degradation, complex manufacturing)
SWAG binding site identified as specific target for Drp1-Fis1 interaction inhibition
Published proof-of-concept in Nature Communications 2023 (Rios et al.) for SC9 mechanism
$388,000 raised through decentralized IP-Token model (Cerebrum DAO + MITY tokens)
Current stage: lead identification and in-vivo testing phase
Team includes Dr. Luis Rios as Co-Founder and Principal Investigator with Stanford research heritage
Mechanism targets fundamental mitochondrial dysfunction rather than downstream symptoms
P110 demonstrated no immunosuppression unlike anti-inflammatory approaches
IP being tokenized and developed with fractional community ownership

milestones.title: P110 Peptide Discovery at Stanford
milestones.description: Stanford researchers developed P110, a peptide designed to block the pathological Drp1–Fis1 interaction while preserving normal mitochondrial dynamics.
milestones.source_quote: Stanford researchers developed P110 – a peptide designed to block the pathological Drp1–Fis1 interaction while preserving normal mitochondrial dynamics (Qi et al., 2013).

milestones.title: P110 Reduces Neurodegeneration in Huntington's Models
milestones.description: P110 demonstrated reduced neurodegeneration in Huntington's disease animal models.
milestones.source_quote: Reduced neurodegeneration in Huntington's disease models (Guo et al., 2013)

milestones.title: P110 Restores Mitochondrial Function in ALS Cells
milestones.description: P110 restored mitochondrial function in ALS patient cells and improved cognitive performance in Alzheimer's models.
milestones.source_quote:
Restored mitochondrial function in ALS patient cells (Joshi et al., 2018)
Improved cognitive performance in Alzheimer's models (Joshi et al., 2018)

milestones.title: P110 Enhances Cardiac Function in Ischemic Disease
milestones.description: P110 demonstrated enhanced cardiac function in ischemic disease models.
milestones.source_quote: Enhanced cardiac function in ischemic disease (Tian et al., 2017)

milestones.title: SWAG Binding Site Identification & SC9 Development
milestones.description: Dr. Luis Rios identified the specific binding site (SWAG – switch I-adjacent groove) and developed SC9, a small molecule mimicking P110's protective effects while overcoming peptide limitations.
milestones.source_quote: Building on years of P110 research, his team developed SC9, a small molecule that mimics P110's protective effects while overcoming peptide limitations (Rios et al., 2023).

milestones.title: Cerebrum DAO Initial Funding Round – $200,000
milestones.description: Cerebrum DAO community funded Fission Bio's initial $200,000 development phase.
milestones.source_quote: The community funded Fission Bio's initial $200,000 development phase

milestones.title: MITY Token IP Tokenization – $188,000 Raised
milestones.description: Intellectual property was tokenized through MITY tokens on the Molecule platform, raising an additional $188,000 for lead identification and in-vivo testing.
milestones.source_quote: then tokenized the intellectual property through MITY tokens on the @Molecule_sci platform, raising an additional $188,000 for lead identification and in-vivo testing.

milestones.title: Total Funding Raised – $400,000
milestones.description: Fission Bio raised a total of $400,000 through Cerebrum DAO's IP-Token model to develop drugs that repair mitochondrial dysfunction.
milestones.source_quote: Fission Bio raised $400,000 through Cerebrum DAO's IP-Token model to develop drugs that repair mitochondrial dysfunction instead of treating downstream symptoms.

milestones.title: FissionBio & Cerebrum DAO 'Meet The Scientists' Podcast
milestones.description: Cerebrum DAO published a 'Meet The Scientists' podcast episode featuring Dr. Luis Rios discussing mitochondria, fission, and the advantages of DeSci funding.
milestones.source_quote: FissionBio @FissionBioDeSci · Aug 27, 2025 Watch @Cerebrum_DAO's latest Meet The Scientists podcast, where @bmagierski talks with FissionBio's Principal Investigator, Dr. Luis Rios.

milestones.title: Lead Identification and In-Vivo Testing Phase
milestones.description: Use of additional $188,000 raised via MITY tokens to conduct lead identification and in-vivo testing of SC9 or related compounds.
milestones.source_quote: raising an additional $188,000 for lead identification and in-vivo testing.

milestones.title: Translation of Compounds into Medicines
milestones.description: Systematic process of turning promising laboratory compounds into viable medicines, the stated next step for Fission Bio.
milestones.source_quote: Fission Bio has proven its concept works in laboratory models. Now comes the systematic process of turning promising compounds into medicines.
